one_line_takeaway: TTP-Detect shows that LLM watermark presence can be audited by a keyless third party via relative hypothesis testing against provider-supplied reference samples, matching a key-dependent baseline on classic biased schemes (e.g., Unigram AUC>=0.999) while remaining measurably weaker on distortion-free schemes (Unbiased/SynthID AUC as low as 0.826).
key_finding: TTP-Detect matches or exceeds the (retrained) UPV baseline on classic logits-based schemes despite operating without any key or scheme-specific detector: e.g., on C4/Llama-3.1-8B, KGW_TTP reaches F1=0.980/AUC=0.998 vs UPV's F1=0.983/AUC=0.991; Unigram_TTP attains AUC>=0.999 across all four model/dataset combinations; SymMark_TTP achieves perfect F1=1.000/AUC=1.000 on every dataset/model combination. Performance degrades on distribution-preserving schemes explicitly engineered to minimize statistical distortion: Unbiased_TTP (AUC 0.826-0.920) and SynthID_TTP (AUC 0.896-0.957) are noticeably weaker than the biased schemes, especially on the out-of-domain OpenGen dataset. Under adversarial attacks on Llama-3.1-8B/C4, TTP-Detect stays robust (e.g., KGW_TTP average AUC 0.980 across attacks), with Dipper-1/Dipper-2 paraphrasing the most disruptive attacks and Unigram_TTP degrading most under paraphrase (attributed to the proxy overfitting to Unigram's fixed vocabulary partition). Reference-set size ablation shows N=16 gives diminishing returns beyond it, with N=8/16 improving average best F1 by >5%/>10% respectively over the reference-free (N=0) setting for distribution-preserving schemes. Module ablation identifies the Adaptive Rank Test as the most influential of the four modules for robustness (largest AUC drops when removed under most attacks), though it can slightly hurt performance under Dipper paraphrasing specifically. Efficiency: TTP-side computation (proxy representation extraction + all four modules) completes in well under 2 seconds per query at N=16 and 300-token generations; the dominant cost is the provider-side reference generation (6.14s on Llama-3.1-8B).
core_contribution: The paper introduces TTP-Detect, a black-box, key-agnostic framework that lets a Trusted Third Party (TTP) auditor determine whether a given text was watermarked by an LLM service provider, WITHOUT access to the provider's secret watermark key, watermarking algorithm, or model internals. It reframes watermark detection (traditionally an absolute-threshold statistic computed by the key-holder) as a relative hypothesis test: given a query text t_q, the TTP queries the provider's black-box API to obtain paired reference sets of watermarked (T_wm) and unwatermarked (T_o) outputs, and asks whether t_q is statistically closer to the watermarked or unwatermarked reference distribution. The pipeline has three stages: (1) a proxy model (LoRA-tuned Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct) is discriminatively fine-tuned on paired watermarked/unwatermarked completions from the provider to produce a representation space that amplifies watermark-relevant differences; (2) four complementary 'relative measurement' modules score the query's affinity to the watermarked reference set relative to the unwatermarked one -- a Local Consistency Test (kernel-weighted density of watermarked neighbors in feature space), a Global Geometry Test (Mahalanobis distance contrast with PCA + shrinkage-regularized covariance, and a non-parametric Energy-distance contrast), and an Adaptive Rank Test (direction-adaptive conformal rank test on token-level NLL global cross-entropy and local volatility, using a separate scoring model Qwen3-1.7B); (3) the four module scores are combined via a logistic-regression ensemble (weights calibrated on an augmented validation set including adversarially perturbed pairs), and a decision threshold tau is calibrated to hit a target false-positive rate on a held-out benign corpus.
threat_model: Three-party setting: a user (U) who submits text of unknown provenance; a model service provider (S) who deploys a proprietary watermarking scheme and exposes only a standard black-box generation API plus a binary watermark on/off flag -- S does not reveal the watermarking mechanism, secret key, or internal model states; and a trusted, regulated third-party auditor (D, the TTP) who must decide whether a submitted text t_q was watermarked by S, using only observable API behavior (D can query S to obtain reference generations but has no access to S's detection pipeline, key, or algorithm). This directly targets the real-world governance/forensics problem that private-key watermark schemes (and even prior 'publicly verifiable' schemes like UPV, PVMark, PDW) still require the verifier to either hold a key or be coupled to the specific injection mechanism, making independent courtroom/regulatory auditing impossible without trusting the provider's own claims.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitation: TTP-Detect's relative measurement modules exploit observable distributional/generative discrepancies between watermarked and unwatermarked outputs, so its gains shrink for watermarking schemes explicitly engineered to minimize bias or distortion (empirically confirmed by the weaker Unbiased/SynthID results relative to biased schemes like KGW/Unigram/SymMark). The paper does not evaluate against an adaptive adversary who knows TTP-Detect's proxy-based methodology and specifically tries to evade it (attacks tested are generic editing/paraphrase, not TTP-Detect-aware). The framework requires the provider S to cooperate by generating reference samples on demand via API, which assumes provider compliance/regulation rather than fully adversarial non-cooperation. Proxy model specialization can backfire (Unigram_TTP's larger robustness drop under paraphrase due to proxy overfitting to Unigram's fixed vocabulary partition). Results are reported on only two provider model families (Llama-3.1-8B, OPT-6.7B) and two datasets (C4, OpenGen).

quantitative_results: KGW_TTP on C4/Llama-3.1-8B: TPR 0.980, TNR 0.980, F1 0.980, AUC 0.998
UPV baseline on C4/Llama-3.1-8B: TPR 0.985, TNR 0.980, F1 0.983, AUC 0.991
Unigram_TTP AUC >= 0.999 across all four (dataset, model) combinations, reaching 1.000 on several
SymMark_TTP: F1=1.000, AUC=1.000 on all datasets and models
SWEET_TTP average AUC drop vs KGW_TTP: 0.38%
Unbiased_TTP AUC range across settings: 0.826 (OpenGen/OPT-6.7B) to 0.920 (C4/OPT-6.7B)
SynthID_TTP AUC range across settings: 0.896 (OpenGen/Llama-3.1-8B) to 0.957 (C4/OPT-6.7B)
Robustness: KGW_TTP average AUC of 0.980 across six attack types (Word-D, Word-S, Word-S-BERT, Dipper-1, Dipper-2, GPT-5.1)
Proxy model ablation: average AUC differs by less than 1% between Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct and Phi-3-mini-4k-instruct proxies
Untuned Qwen2.5-3B-Base proxy: AUC drop below 4% relative to instruction-tuned proxy for SymMark_TTP and Unigram_TTP
Reference set size: N=8 and N=16 improve average best F1 by >5% and >10% respectively over N=0 for distribution-preserving schemes (Unbiased, SynthID)

Efficiency: reference-set generation on Llama-3.1-8B takes 6.1374s; TTP-side representation extraction (Qwen2.5-3B) 0.8348s; Local Consistency Test 0.0012s; Mahalanobis 0.0165s; Energy 0.0113s; Adaptive Rank Test (Qwen3-1.7B) 1.7328s; total TTP-side computation under 2s
baselines_compared: UPV (Liu et al., 2024a) - retrained by authors since no public checkpoints for their chosen models/datasets; the only prior black-box/publicly-detectable watermark detector used as an empirical comparison. PVMark and PDW/Fairoze et al. are discussed but explicitly excluded as baselines because PVMark is a ZKP-verifiability plugin for a keyed detector (not standalone key-free detection) and PDW requires cooperative signature-embedding design changes, both orthogonal to this paper's black-box parameter-free third-party detection setting.
